author: @darthlaurel
text:
I have to say that if I went to this and discovered it was a musical, I'd be livid. I thoroughly hate that genre and being hoodwinked into attending one would make me very angry.
I've listened to a lot of people talk about this movie but you are the first person who has said it is a musical. Wth is going on with that?

replies.author: @dlfendel2844

author: @dlfendel2844
text: Mostly agree-- watched the screener. I'd say the director's visual style was balanced to the negative by his encouragement or toleration of the largely Brit character actor cast's shameless overthetop scenery chewing which makes this into a kind of on-film version of a Brit pantomime play that jars against Chalamet and the little girl's more realistic perfs. As for the musical numbers? I counted two that were pretty good, the rest being in that "High School Musical/American Idol Tryout" school of over-warbled recitative lyrics that go nowhere other than to repeat the same tired themes over and over again with some not-bad/not-great choreography and lots of CGI enhancement. This makes those other songs tedious and endless, not charming. Grant has given interviews saying making this movie was a pain and annoying and his perf in a very GOOD way shows that feeling-- talk about method! Atkinson is of course great but used too little. It IS interesting that the ORIGINAL movie was shot at studios in Bavaria and in Germany which explained the uniforms, Germanic settings, etc. etc. but this was done in the UK but keeps that mittel-europaische germanic style for its own bizarre and unknowable reasons. I'm also confused by the credits mentioning the Quebec tax break scheme, but maybe that's where the CGI work was done. I haven't got little kids, but somehow I think if --I-- were a little one I'd get bored a LOT during this movie. VR did a review suggesting it will grow in boxoffice the way "The Greatest Showman" did and I disagree because of course (a) that was for adults and (b) I think the word of mouth for the emptyness and less-than-promised or different-than-promised points you made will kill it quickly. It is doing well in a vacuum, after all, with no Disney holiday stuff to compete. I also think this movie might just help Dune II because it will intro Chalamet to new audiences (me for instance) but....  Finally, the ending use of "Pure Imagination" (not counting the famous intro used at the beginning and during the film to hint to you the origins, which shows how powerful that three note intro is) is great, but it shows just how weak all the other musical numbers are by comparison. You know the old saying about if you attack the King you have to kill the King? Well, the same goes for spin-offs and origin movies and the rest when the first original epic is SO well known and SO beloved.  This doesn't make it, and I agree with your ratings totally.

author: @elskeletor3566
text: I'd say Gene Wilder's comment on Tim Burton's Charlie and the Chocolate Factory applies to this movie.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: Well Gene was really at odds with the remake. To quote,  "It's just some people sitting around thinking, 'How can we make some more money?' Why else would you remake Willy Wonka? I don't see the point of going back and doing it all over again. I like Johnny Depp and I appreciate that he has said on the record that my shoes would be hard to fill. But I don't know how it will all turn out. Right now, the only thing that does take some of the edge off this for me is that Willy Wonka's name isn't in the title."

author: @erik1836
text:
At this point in our journey across the barren landscape, the Sahara desert bereft of creativity that is Hollywood one is far safer betting that things like this remake of someone else's masterpiece is going to be more like eating week old left overs that have been sitting in the frig - not yet poisonously spoiled but, the next best thing to it being so. 

We cannot have, evidence, true creativity by allowing ourselves to march to the beat of a long dead and buried drummer - who, in his time was a master, a genius, but now no more.

Basically, the directors, actors etc of today are nothing much better than - take the money and run - hoes; happy to sell themselves to the highest bidder.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: Well said. We are now suffering a dearth of talent in all aspects of entertainment production.

author: @jps7960
text: Many critics were hoping that this movie would flop. However, the opposite happened.The movie was great from beginning to end. It has already made over $560 million dollars.. I would say that to huge success.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text:
It actually depends on your definition of the word "Huge". All in that we know about for the film is a $125M production budget (Likely higher) and a promotion budget minimum of $125M. All in we will just take the $250M expenditure as is. The rake of the cinemas around the world is roughly 50% average. Using your number (which I believe is actually low since I am almost certain it hit $600M on it's competition-less legs), the film potentially made near $60M. Which is a feat since most films are failing.

What's even more surprising is it got an unexpected second wind 2 weeks after debut, beating out mathematical modeling. Whoever windowed this needs a hefty bonus for booking and good luck. Important to remember that the Ghostbusters sequel was originally slated at the same time. There is also the minor note of Aquaman 2 just outright failing to pull any audience interest. Seriously hats off to whoever did that.

So as I always try to do, I'll say this film may have managed $100M in profit to date based on my higher $600M number, yet my guess is due to incentives for Chalmette and others in the film that isn't even a given. For me the odd part is the 2 films I missed on for financial estimations that did well, they were both Warner Brothers. They really needed those winners too. Trying to figure the losses for the studio would be even more challenging as the slate of DC offerings was a series of failures.

Cheers!

author: @JWS1313
text: I am probably the odd man out here on the fact I am not going to see the second "Dune" (if you can really call it that) movie.  As a fan of the Dune Books (well the first three at least) they got so much stuff wrong in the first ten minutes I had to walk away from the movie despite how visually stunning it was.  If it wasn't supposed to be Dune I guess I might have enjoyed it; but sadly it was supposed to be Dune and wasn't. I also have the same feelings about the Jackson Lord of the Rings and Hobbit movies.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: Strong take. I get the Dune reaction. Jackson never should have sullied his reputation with the hobbit films

replies.author: @JWS1313
replies.text: @CultureCasino Alot of the characters on the Jackson film feel like they are bipolar or something the side characters of Theoden and Denethor feel that way to me. For example the scene where Theoden asks Aragorn why should we march to the aid of those who did not come to our aid. The beacons are lit, let's ride to Gondor. There is no explanation in the movie why Theoden suddenly changed his mind and I can only come to the conclusion that the Jackson version of Theoden is bipolar or some kind of mental. And that is just one of many things wrong with these films. And in the case of Denethor I do not care how many people tell me that line in the movie is better it is not better.  You cannot convince me that Flee for your lives is better then the line in the book of Denether telling them, "Go back to your posts and burn."

author: @GraybeardSkydaddy
text:
Um, editing. What's up with criticizing the state-of-the-art graphics of AwkwardMan 2? Isn't shocking fluidity the goal? Does everything not flow with liquidity and grace? I mean, much of it was completed very recently, up until last week, no? Is this not the modern stroke of genius, to complete the movie in post right up until the day of release?

Poor Timmy Shalamee, Shallamet, Challengeme, the Dune guy. Thing about a Wonka movie, who asked for it? Who decided it was a good time for it? Why? 

I went to see a movie last night. My sister really wanted me to go. I bought a ticket, a Coke and a pretzel. I feel ripped off now, because that brief experience was over $25, and I did not receive a $25 experience. I am much more satisfied here at home in my gaming chair facing my monitors, and watching an unlimited number of things from the past and present.

text: So I gotta ask: Has Chalamet done something controversial, or I suppose "woke"? Because it is pretty obvious that the guys from this circle are constantly laying it thick on him.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: I'm pretty sure I gave him praise throughout this review. Including his ability to sing.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: I think I gave it more than a fair review here. I think people may be walking into this film unprepared for it being a musical. It is a middle of the road film.

replies.author: @oscarchavez8527
replies.text: @CultureCasino  Oh I know, I know bud. I was just curious as if he had done something outside the film. Like, one time you lay it on hard on Chris Evans because the dude said some dumbshit at some point. So I was just curious as if Chalamet had done something similar, that is all. Nothing but curiosity, that's all.
